an7.61:1.2: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Bhaggas, at Susumāragiri, in Bhesakaḷā Grove, in the deer park.
an7.61:1.3: Now at that time Venerable Mahāmoggallāna was sitting among the Magadhans, in Kallavāḷaputta village, drowsing.
an7.61:1.4: The *Bhagavā* saw, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna sitting among the Magadhans in Kallavāḷaputta village, drowsing.
an7.61:1.6: just as a strong man might extend his bent arm or bend his extended arm, so too the *Bhagavā* vanished from Bhesakaḷā Grove, in the deer park at Susumāragiri among the Bhaggas, and appeared before Venerable Mahāmoggallāna in Kallavāḷaputta village among the Magadhans.
an7.61:1.7: The *Bhagavā* sat down on the seat laid out.
an7.61:1.8: Seated, the *Bhagavā* said this to Venerable Mahāmoggallāna:
an7.61:10.1: Therefore, Moggallāna, you should train thus:
an7.61:10.2: ‘I will not approach families with my nose held high.’
an7.61:10.3: This is how you should train, Moggallāna.
an7.61:10.4: For if a *bhikkhu*, Moggallāna, approaches families with his nose held high, there are tasks to be done in families.
an7.61:10.5: Because of those, people do not pay attention to a *bhikkhu* who has arrived. Then this occurs to the *bhikkhu*:
an7.61:10.6: ‘Who has now turned this family against me? These people now seem disinclined toward me.’
an7.61:10.7: So from lack of gain comes dejection; from dejection, restlessness; for one who is restless, lack of restraint; for one unrestrained, the mind is far from *samādhi*.
an7.61:11.1: Therefore, Moggallāna, you should train thus:
an7.61:11.3: This is how you should train, Moggallāna.
an7.61:11.4: For when there is contentious talk, Moggallāna, much talk is to be expected; when there is much talk, there is restlessness; for one who is restless, lack of restraint; for one unrestrained, the mind is far from *samādhi*.
an7.61:11.5: I do not praise every kind of association, Moggallāna.
an7.61:11.6: But neither do I say that no kind of association is praiseworthy, Moggallāna.
an7.61:11.7: Association with householders and with those gone forth—that I do not praise, Moggallāna.
an7.61:11.8: But as for those lodgings that are quiet, noiseless, secluded, hidden from people, suitable for retreat, association with lodgings of that kind I praise.”
an7.61:12.1: When this was said, Venerable Mahāmoggallāna said this to the *Bhagavā*:
an7.61:12.2: “To what extent, bhante, is a *bhikkhu*, in brief, liberated through the destruction of *taṇhā*, one of ultimate fulfillment, ultimate security from bondage, ultimate holy life, ultimate consummation, foremost among gods and humans?”
an7.61:13.10: To this extent, Moggallāna, a *bhikkhu*, in brief, is liberated through the destruction of *taṇhā*, one of ultimate fulfillment, ultimate security from bondage, ultimate holy life, ultimate consummation, foremost among gods and humans.”
an7.61:13.2: ‘All *dhammas* are not fit to be clung to.’
an7.61:13.3: And when, Moggallāna, a *bhikkhu* has heard this:
an7.61:13.4: ‘All *dhammas* are not fit to be clung to,’
an7.61:13.5: he directly knows every *dhamma*; having directly known every *dhamma*, he fully understands every *dhamma*. Having fully understood every *dhamma*, whatever *vedanā* he feels—whether *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*—
an7.61:13.6: he dwells contemplating as impermanent [*aniccānupassī*] with regard to those *vedanās*, contemplating fading away [*virāgānupassī*], contemplating cessation [*nirodhānupassī*], contemplating relinquishment [*paṭinissaggānupassī*].
an7.61:13.7: Dwelling thus, contemplating as impermanent [*aniccānupassī*], contemplating fading away [*virāgānupassī*], contemplating cessation [*nirodhānupassī*], contemplating relinquishment [*paṭinissaggānupassī*] with regard to those *vedanās*, he does not cling [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
an7.61:13.8: Not clinging [*anupādiyaṁ*], he is not agitated; being unagitated, he personally attains final *nibbāna*.
an7.61:13.9: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
an7.61:2.1: “Are you drowsing, Moggallāna? Are you drowsing, Moggallāna?”
an7.61:2.3: “Therefore, Moggallāna, with whatever *saññā* you are dwelling when that drowsiness comes over you, do not attend to that *saññā*; do not cultivate that *saññā* much.
an7.61:2.4: It is possible, Moggallāna, that as you dwell thus that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:3.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should keep thinking [*anuvitakkeyyāsi*] and examining [*anuvicāreyyāsi*] the *dhamma* as heard and as learned, and review it mentally [*anupekkheyyāsi*].
an7.61:3.2: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:4.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should recite the *dhamma* as heard and as learned in detail.
an7.61:4.2: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:5.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should pull both your ears and rub your limbs with your hand.
an7.61:5.2: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:6.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should rise from your seat, wash your eyes with water, look around in all directions, and look up at the constellations and the stars.
an7.61:6.2: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:7.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should attend to the *saññā* of light and establish the *saññā* of daytime—
an7.61:7.2: as by day, so by night; as by night, so by day.
an7.61:7.3: Thus, with the mind [*cetasā*] open and unobstructed, you should develop [*bhāveyyāsi*] a luminous mind [*citta*].
an7.61:7.4: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:8.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should determine on walking back and forth, aware of what is behind and before, with the *indriyas* drawn inward, with the mind not gone outward.
an7.61:8.2: It is possible that, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would be abandoned.
an7.61:9.1: If, as you dwell thus, that drowsiness would not be abandoned, then, Moggallāna, you should lie down in the lion’s posture on your right side, placing one foot upon the other, with *sati*, with full awareness [*sampajāno*], attending to the *saññā* of rising.
an7.61:9.2: And when you awaken, Moggallāna, you should get up at once quickly:
an7.61:9.3: ‘I will not dwell devoted to the pleasure of lying down, the pleasure of reclining, or the pleasure of drowsiness.’
an7.61:9.4: This is how you should train, Moggallāna.
